title: N-Grams & Text Preprocessing
concept: Preprocessing (lowercasing, removing punctuation, removing stopwords, and stemming or lemmatizing words to their root form) cleans up text before vectorizing, while N-grams group adjacent words like 'not good' into a single feature so a flip in meaning isn't lost the way plain Bag of Words would lose it.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In TF-IDF, what happens to extremely common words like "the" and "is"?

options.text: Their weight gets pushed down close to zero, since they don't distinguish one document from another
Their weight gets boosted, since they appear so often
They get deleted from the document entirely before any weighting happens

exercises.explain: Right! TF-IDF pushes common filler words toward zero. Today: cleaning up text even before that step, and fixing a sneaky blind spot word counts alone can't catch.

cards.title: "Not good" vs. "good"
cards.body: A review says "This movie was NOT good." A Bag of Words vector just counts "not" once and "good" once — the same two counts you'd get from a review that only said "good." The single word that flips the entire meaning gets completely lost.

cards.title: Preprocessing and N-grams
cards.body: Preprocessing cleans raw text before it's vectorized: lowercasing (so "Great" and "great" count as one word), stripping punctuation, removing stopwords (common filler like "the," "is," "and" that adds little signal), and stemming or lemmatization (chopping words down to a shared root, so "running," "runs," and "ran" all collapse into "run"). An N-gram then groups N adjacent words into a single feature — a 2-word bigram like "not_good" is kept together instead of being split apart.

cards.title: Splitting "New York" in half
cards.body: If you chopped the phrase "New York" into two separate words, "New" and "York," you'd lose the city entirely — neither word alone means the same thing. That's exactly the problem with treating "not" and "good" as two unrelated words. An N-gram keeps tightly linked words glued together as one unit, the same way "New York" only makes sense as a pair.

cards.title: Cleaning a review, step by step
cards.body: Raw: "The movie was NOT very good!!!" Lowercase + strip punctuation: "the movie was not very good". Remove stopwords: "movie not very good". Now add bigrams alongside single words: "not_very" and "very_good" become their own features, so the classifier can finally see that "not" is attached to "good" — not floating free.

cards.title: Word counts alone don't catch negation
cards.body: It's easy to assume that once you're counting words, sentiment is basically solved. In reality, a plain unigram (single-word) Bag of Words or TF-IDF vector treats "not" and "good" as two completely independent counts — it has no way to know "not" is standing right next to "good" and flipping it. Only grouping adjacent words together, as an N-gram does, fixes that.

cards.title: Unigrams vs. Bigrams
cards.body: Two words, kept apart or kept together:

exercises.prompt: What does stemming or lemmatization do to a word like "running"?

options.text: Reduces it to a shared root form, like "run", so variants of the same word don't each get their own separate vocabulary entry
Translates it into a completely different language
Deletes it from the vocabulary entirely

exercises.explain: Right — stemming/lemmatization collapses word variants like "running," "runs," and "ran" down to one shared root, keeping the vocabulary from ballooning.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Removing stopwords like "the" and "is" before vectorizing usually helps a classifier focus on more meaningful words.
exercises.explain: True — stopwords add bulk without much signal, so clearing them out ahead of time gives the model a cleaner set of features to learn from.

exercises.sentence: A sequence of two adjacent words treated as a single feature, like "not_good", is called a ___.
exercises.explain: A bigram is an N-gram with N=2 — two adjacent words grouped together as one unit instead of being counted apart.

exercises.prompt: Which choice best explains why a bigram like "not_amazing" captures more meaning than treating "not" and "amazing" as separate unigrams?

options.text: Keeping the words glued together preserves the fact that "not" is directly flipping "amazing", which two separate counts can't show
Bigrams are always shorter than unigrams, so they're faster to compute
Bigrams remove the need for a vocabulary entirely

exercises.explain: Exactly — the whole value of an N-gram is that it keeps meaningfully linked words stuck together instead of scattering them into independent counts.

exercises.prompt: A review reads: "the food was not amazing." Using only single-word (unigram) Bag of Words, a classifier would separately count "not" once and "amazing" once. Why might it mistakenly lean toward classifying this as a positive review, and how does adding bigrams fix that?

options.text: "Amazing" alone looks positive to the unigram counts, but a bigram feature like "not_amazing" keeps the negation attached to the word it's flipping, letting the classifier see the true negative meaning
The classifier would correctly read this as negative even with unigrams alone, since it understands English grammar
Bigrams fix this by deleting the word "amazing" from the vocabulary entirely
This isn't actually a problem, since word order never matters for classification

exercises.explain: Exactly right! Unigrams see "amazing" and miss that "not" is standing right next to it. Bigrams catch what plain word counts can't. You now have every piece of the puzzle — time to put it all together into a real classifier.
